title: Ursula Le Guin's Speculative Anthropology: Thick Description, Historicity and Science Fiction
author: Davison-Vecchione and Seeger

opening_move.description: Le Guin's science fiction has been insufficiently theorized as 'speculative anthropology'. The paper proposes this concept to capture how her work combines Geertz's thick description with a Jamesonian historicism — overcoming the limitations of each through their synthesis.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Reading Le Guin as speculative anthropology both rehabilitates her work against Jameson's 'world reduction' charge and illuminates science fiction's capacity to disclose utopian possibilities within the present.

rivals_engaged: Jameson's 'world reduction' critique of Le Guin
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper uses Jameson's critique of Le Guin as the foil to motivate a synthesis of thick description (Geertz) and historicity (Jameson) that Le Guin's work is argued to achieve more successfully than either theorist alone.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Literary close reading and hermeneutic analysis: detailed textual analysis of Le Guin's The Left Hand of Darkness and The Dispossessed, read through Geertz's anthropological method and Jameson's Marxist theory of utopia.
epistemic_mode.notes: Le Guin's novels are the primary object of interpretation. The argument is literary-theoretical: close reading demonstrates the thesis about speculative anthropology as method.

case_type.case_name: Ursula Le Guin — The Left Hand of Darkness and The Dispossessed

normative_stake.domain: theory of utopia, science fiction studies, anthropological method

confidence_notes: The paper's epistemic mode is normative_with_interpretive_case where the 'case' is Le Guin's science fiction novels, read through close literary-theoretical analysis. The argument is about the methodological contribution of Le Guin's work. The case is the primary object of interpretation, not an illustration of a prior theory.
